title: Traveling with Kyle - Agentina Part 3, Uruguay, Brazil Part 1

transcript.text: Yeah, so Argentina, yeah, I also remember the people were, like, protesting something that the government did.
I guess they were all blaming it.
They get pissed off at the government, and so they would all, they all, like, made these huge bonfires in every single intersection of the whole city.
They made these bonfires that had flames, like, 30 feet high, and they pretty much, and everybody was taking part.
They were, like, all the cars were, like, honking all these people.
At one point, there was this march where all the people were just walking down the roads, thousands of people, you know, elbow to elbow, banging on pots and pans, protesting something.
And another point I saw, an interesting parade, kind of outdoor, next to some Congress building, I guess it was, like, homosexual or transvestite parade,
And there was, like, all these guys standing around with perfect tits, their shirts off, and girls, too.
But, yeah, interesting times there in Argentina.
So, anyways, I was there two months, and I was, like, all right, well, told the Institute, okay, well, if you can't pay me, you know, two months after, I'll do it.
What I'll do is I'll go travel for two months, and then I'll come back, and maybe you can pay me that.
So, I go and I travel, I took the boat across to Uruguay, and I stayed in a hotel in Uruguay, and Uruguay was really similar to Argentina, pretty much the white people, just like Argentina.
I guess Buenos Aires, most of Argentina, what I could tell, were pretty much, I guess, maybe half the people or something, or maybe even most of them were white.
Like, they were immigrants from Italy and Spain, and even when I would, like, ride my bike through town, and I'd hear people talk, it was pretty much like they were talking Italian.
They spoke with the same, that's what it sounded like to me.
It sounded Italian to me.
And, yeah, I went, saw the neighborhood to the east, and it was kind of almost, like, American-style neighborhood, nice houses and stuff, nice bike path going along.
But, yeah, so I go into Uruguay, and it was pretty much the same kinds of people, like, white kind of people, kind of similar accents and stuff.
But they would, they insisted that they were more similar to Brazil than to Argentina.
They liked Brazil more than Argentina.
I guess Argentinians, kind of, everybody in South America doesn't really like them.
They kind of like the odd man out or something.
But then I took the bus into some other town, which I guess, I think it's the capital of Uruguay.
I can't remember what it's called, Montevideo.
And it used to have the tallest building in all of South America there that I looked at.
And I guess this was in the 30s because it was only, like, 10 stories high or something.
But they had a nice beach, but I didn't check it out.
I stayed there, like, six days there or something because I had to get my visa for Brazil.
Brazil's, like, the only country in South America where you actually have to get a visa to get there.
It was, like, 60 bucks I had to pay.
And it was only for, like, two months.
But, yeah, they were really friendly Uruguayans.
A lot friendlier than Argentinians.
I guess they weren't having that crisis, so they were not as grumpy.
But they also liked to talk and stuff.
But, yeah, they identified more with Brazil.
But the southern part of Brazil is, like, white people, too.
It's kind of like Argentina and Uruguay.
But, yeah, and then I went on to some, I can't remember what it's called, like a beach resort place in Montevideo.
But before that, I went to some other town and stayed at some small town.
I don't even remember why.
I had a cool tower that I overlooked a place.
I stayed at some hotel, but this Irish guy, and he was telling me about, like, he didn't really like Uruguay.
He said, like, it was corrupt, like, everybody, the government there made everybody a general
So that people could get general pay and not have to do any work.
It was kind of, he's kind of like one of these expatriates who's moved to a country, but they hate the country they live in, I guess.
I guess they forget that why they moved, they left their own country in the first place or something.
Kind of reminded me of my mom's friends in Kathmandu, who's lived there for, like, 20 years,
But only had negative things to say about the country.
But, yeah, he said that before the devaluation in Buenos Aires, I guess it happened right after I left,
That all the people who controlled Buenos Aires or Argentina were carting money out of the banks for themselves
So that they wouldn't have to pay for the devaluation in semi-18-wheeler loads of cash.
Um, but, uh, anyways, then I took the bus into some southern town in Brazil.
And the Brazil, I noticed that in Brazil, the towns were, like, huge.
Brazil's, like, the fifth most populated country in the world, I think.
It's also the fifth largest country in the world by size.
It's bigger than Australia.
But most of Brazil is the jungle, so all that population is in the cities, and these cities are huge.
I mean, I wouldn't be surprised if Brazil has the most cities over a million people than anywhere in the world other than America.
Um, I remember just taking the bus into these cities and just driving forever across these things.
They're huge. Like, they're all as big as Buenos Aires.
And I went to one city, and the biggest one, I can't remember what it's called, but it had a really nice pedestrian mall in the center of it, and it looked really upscale.
It looked like they were better off economically than Argentina or Uruguay.
And, um, they had, like, a nice train that rolled through, and so the subway down below was up above, and then I took another, oh, to Florianopolis.
I spent a night, and there were lots of prostitutes there.
Uh, ooh, I probably should have hired one. I didn't tell.
Um, and there was a nice beach, a nice surf beach there, though, but I didn't surf there.
And it was, like, in its own little, little, uh, island.
And I went to another couple towns. It's kind of, like, staying in the town and walking around it, and there was really good food.
I liked the food in Brazil. It was, the food in Argentina was pretty good, but it was, like, like, more fried,
Whereas the food in Brazil was just really healthy, wholesome food, like vegetables and all this cool stuff just thrown in there in big portions,
And it wasn't as expensive. I got a cool vibe from Brazil at the beginning. It was cool.
Then I took the bus into, um, Sao Paulo, which is, like, this giant city.
I guess it's one of the biggest cities in the world, like, almost 20 million people.
Right up there, a little bit higher altitude, right in the desert. I mean, right in the jungle.
And I took, I got off into the bus station, and the bus station was the biggest bus station I've ever seen in my life.
It was gigantic, multi-level, where you go get the tickets. It's, like, 20, you know, ticket booths deep.
And some of these Brazilian, these Brazilian journalists had given me their number,
And they wanted me to stay with them there, the ones I met in Uruguay, but for some reason I didn't call them.
I guess I just wanted to pass through Sao Paulo.
But I took the, uh, it was interesting, in Sao Paulo, um, the, uh, subway, right there in the, uh, in the, um, bus station,
The subway was actually, like, on the second story.
So you actually had to go up to get on the subway, and then it went down below.
That was pretty cool.
So, so I took it into, at first, I think, the first place I took it in was, uh, the downtown area, the strip of skyscrapers.
And I walked down that a little bit, and it looked, it looked, uh, peaceful and, like, upscale, you know, safe and everything.
And I had a meal there.
And then I took the subway into the historical downtown.
A lot of cities in South America have two downtowns.
They have the, uh, historical downtown and then the, uh, the new downtown.
I guess, I guess they, uh, the historical downtown gets run down,
And people try to start a new downtown for the business people,
And then don't, so they don't have to deal with all the street people or whatever who moved into the downtown.
So it makes downtown really cheap, and it's just kind of weird, especially in Sao Paulo,
Because I got off at the subway, and it was, like, some kind of Ironman getting bombed out third world, like, hellhole in the middle of Sao Paulo.
I got out of, like, there was their central plaza, and I get out from the subway, right from the central plaza,
And I step out, and they're, like, the cops are putting a body into their cock car.
And there's just lots of street vendors and kind of, like, street urchins all over the place.
But it was beautiful because there was this, uh, there was this really narrow pedestrian mall that went north,
And then it opened up into this really big area because where it hit another pedestrian mall,
Like, crisscrossed, just like in Buenos Aires, and then got really wide as a pedestrian mall with a big, like, fountain there,
And it was really beautiful.
It was a great downtown, but it was just deserted, kind of.
There were no people there.
There were just, like, street people there, and it was kind of a bummer.
Oh, I'm out of time.
